decision: Slice 08e-2 is ACCEPTABLE AFTER one P2 fix. The splice correctly removes the canonical commit from the implementation worker path (the if pending_merge_queue: early-return always returns before _commit_repos at implementation.py:15968), fails closed on every dependency gap with _MergeQueueEnqueueError -> workflow_blocked and never falls back to the legacy commit, preserves the post-DAG verify gates and the post-test guard (only the existing early-return body was replaced; no new early return; nothing reordered), and keeps the three commit helpers as untouched doc-08-step-7 compat shims. The 2 updated tests preserve original intent (the canonical-mutation named test still monkeypatches _commit_repos to assert-not-run and still asserts repair routing does not run; the shared _assert_pending_merge_queue_blocker helper keeps its structural assertions and only relaxes the blocker-message string to accept the genuine new fail-closed family). The 4 new real-Postgres tests genuinely exercise the happy path, enqueue idempotency, and two fail-closed modes. The P2: the phase-3 per-task enqueue loop is not transactionally atomic, so a mid-loop failure orphans the already-enqueued lanes while the un-enqueued tasks are never retried by any resume path -> a permanently stuck multi-task group, contradicting doc-08 checkpoint atomicity. Fix by wrapping the phase-3 loop in one transaction, or by making resume re-drive the enqueue for tasks lacking a merge_queue_items row. The three modeling questions are all acceptable per doc 08: per-task lanes are an isolation-safe conservative choice (group lanes are an optional optimization); 08e-2-only enqueue-then-block is the correct intermediate state of an atomic construction order; the aggregate_verdict pre-queue node is the correct durable aggregate attestation (the real contract gate ran at sandbox capture, the post-apply gates run in the queue worker).

Adding a transaction-aware enqueue variant or a bulk-enqueue method to MergeQueueStore (unnecessary -- asyncpg nests Connection.transaction() as a SAVEPOINT on an already-open connection, so wrapping the loop in one outer transaction makes the existing per-call enqueue atomic with zero change to MergeQueueStore; adding a method would be the over-engineered path)

summary: Slice 08e-3a REVIEW (independent, review-only): the durable merge queue DRAIN worker (_drain_durable_merge_queue_for_feature, _drain_one_merge_queue_lane, _merge_queue_post_apply_gate_decision, _route_merge_queue_drain_failure + _MERGE_QUEUE_DRAIN_FAILURE_PAIRS) plus the 9-test test_merge_queue_drain.py, all additive in implementation.py. Zero P1, THREE P2, one P3. P2-1: a RAISED worker exception (LeaseFencedError / MergeQueueError / asyncpg error from transition/acquire_feature_lock/advance_repo_target) escapes apply_candidate / run_required_gates / commit_and_prove_clean -- which are called with no try/except at implementation.py:5759/5777/5793 (only mark_integrated is wrapped) -- aborts the whole drain loop and strands every still-queued sibling lane, contradicting the stated 'a sibling failure does not strand the others' contract (true only for RETURNED applied=False failures); worker methods do not heartbeat + 5-min claim TTL makes a slow lane hit a fenced transition. P2-2: the post-apply conflict-marker gate runs `git diff --check` (implementation.py:5629) which inspects the UNSTAGED worktree, but apply_candidate stages the applied patch via `git apply --index` -- the gate misses STAGED conflict markers and falsely approves; working_tree_clean already checks both --quiet and --cached --quiet, proving the codebase knows the applied content is staged; the rejects-conflict-markers test only exercises an unstaged worktree so it masks the defect. P2-3: the drain wires claim but never recover_expired, and claim's queued/expired-leased predicate never re-takes an applying/verifying/committing row -- so a lane that crashes after canonical apply starts is invisible to a drain re-run and never recovered, which doc 08 Lease Semantics + the Rollback And Recovery Table explicitly require; no crash/re-drive test among the 9. P3-1: a drain contract_violation maps to (contract_violation,outside_allowed_paths) which is run_product_repair with allow_product_repair=True, but the routed observation carries no target_paths/contract_ids so decide() downgrades it to quiesce -- fail-closed and safe (Slice 07 non-negotiable), but a genuine contract escape cannot be scoped-repaired. Vector 5 (post-apply gate) VERDICT: a real gap, P2 -- not because git diff --check is minimal but because it inspects the wrong (unstaged) tree state. Vector 6 (deferred _implement_dag invocation) VERDICT: a sound split, NOT a half-wired hazard -- the drain has no call site, doc 08 ss 12-15 + IMPLEMENTATION_PROMPT 180-182 sanction intermediate states behind the Slice 12 gate, and the 08e-2 enqueue side is itself uninvoked so there is no enqueue-with-no-drainer hazard; caveat: P2-3's recovery wiring must land in 08e-3b with the call site. Vectors 1/2/4/7 PASS: claim/drive/reload ordering matches the 08d worker contract; lease fencing genuinely prevents double-apply (no TOCTOU); all 7 failure-class route pairs present in ROUTE_TABLE and no path silently swallows a failure or falls back to _commit_group/direct commit; the post-DAG verify gates + post-test guard are untouched. Verdict: 08e-3a acceptable after the 3 P2 fixes.

decision: Slice 08e-3a is ACCEPTABLE AFTER three P2 fixes; zero P1. The drain's claim/drive/reload loop is correct and matches the Slice 08d worker contract, the lease fencing on every transition/advance_repo_target genuinely prevents a double-apply (a second claim cannot take an applying+ lane; a stale-version transition is fenced), no path silently falls back to the legacy _commit_group/direct commit, all 7 failure-class route pairs are present in ROUTE_TABLE and the record/decide/mark_route_started cycle fires, and the post-DAG verify gates + post-test guard are untouched. The three P2s: (P2-1) wrap each worker-method call in _drain_one_merge_queue_lane in try/except funneling into _fail_result so a RAISED failure is recorded+routed like a returned one and the loop continues -- as written a fenced/transient error on one lane aborts the drain and strands siblings; (P2-2) the post-apply gate must run `git diff --check HEAD` or add `git diff --cached --check` because apply_candidate's `git apply --index` stages the applied content and bare `git diff --check` inspects only the unstaged worktree, missing staged conflict markers; (P2-3) 08e-3b MUST wire recover_expired (or document the recovery owner) plus a crash-after-apply re-drive test, since the drain only wires claim and claim never re-takes an applying/verifying/committing lane. Vector 5 verdict: the git diff --check post-apply gate is a real gap (P2-2) -- it inspects the wrong tree state, not merely a minimal one. Vector 6 verdict: the deferred _implement_dag invocation is a sound split and NOT a half-wired hazard -- the drain has no call site, doc 08 sanctions intermediate construction states behind the Slice 12 gate, and the 08e-2 enqueue side is itself uninvoked so there is no live enqueue-with-no-drainer hazard; P2-3's recovery wiring must land in 08e-3b with the call site. P3-1 (drain contract_violation dead-ends at quiesce for lack of scoped evidence -- fail-closed and safe) folds into 08e-3b/08f.

Raising P2-2 (post-apply gate wrong tree state) to P1 -- a genuine 3-way conflict is already caught by apply_patch's non-zero exit inside apply_candidate, and the drain is uninvoked so there is no live miss; the gate is ineffective as advertised but not currently unsafe, so P2

summary: Slice 08e-3b — wire the durable merge queue drain into _implement_dag + splice the post-DAG checkpoint. The final 08e integration sub-slice: makes the queue LIVE end-to-end (enqueue -> drain -> checkpoint). Two coupled additive changes in implementation.py, no working 08d/08e-1/08e-2/08e-3a code refactored. (A) The if pending_merge_queue: block of _implement_dag (implementation.py:16903) now calls _drain_durable_merge_queue_for_feature right after the 08e-2 enqueue (drain call at implementation.py:16992) so the queued task:{id} lanes are driven to integrated; a drain module-unavailable error or any non-integrated lane fails the group closed via the new local _merge_queue_group_blocked helper (typed workflow_blocked, never the legacy commit). (B) New additive function _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group (implementation.py:6259) replaces the legacy _commit_group/_record_dag_group_commit_proof/_project_dag_group_checkpoint path for the queue-driven flow: it builds the Slice 08d GroupMergeCoordinator (MergeQueueStore + build_checkpoint_projector wiring + a deterministic ExpectedTaskIdsProvider) and runs coverage + checkpoint_group, which projects the single dag-group:{group_idx} artifact via ExecutionControlStore.project_group_checkpoint (projection_owner=merge_queue) and advances every covered lane integrated->done. The block calls it after the drain (implementation.py:17048); on success it records handover successes, deletes the stale pending markers, logs dag_merge_queue_group_checkpointed, refreshes the public exhibit, then group_idx += 1; continue. The legacy _verify_and_fix_group checkpoint (the real site located by reading at implementation.py ~15281-15487 — doc 08 step-5 lines 4166/4203 are stale) is byte-for-byte unchanged and runs only for the non-queue legacy path; _commit_repos/_commit_group/_record_dag_group_commit_proof kept as doc-08-step-7 compat shims, no longer called on the queue path. Checkpoint/coordinator failures route via _route_merge_queue_drain_failure as checkpoint_contradiction -> checkpoint_after_failed_gate (a deterministic fail-closed quiesce route), the same typed way 08e-3a routes drain failures. Post-DAG verify gates (execute() Step 2+ code review/QA/security/verifier) + the post_test_observation.py post-test guard are preserved — they run after _implement_dag returns complete, which the queue path can now reach (08e-2 always returned workflow_blocked); not bypassed/reordered. The durable queue path is NOT runtime-gated (doc 08: any flag is a startup readiness guard only; verify_merge_queue_production_ready is it); it is selected per-result by the sandbox-set pending_durable_merge_queue note — default-on whenever Slice 04 sandbox capture is active. New test file test_merge_queue_checkpoint.py: 6 end-to-end real-Postgres tests (enqueue -> drain -> checkpoint) covering single-task, multi-task, idempotent re-run, coverage-not-approved routed, failed-drained-lane routed, and fail-closed-without-store.

summary: Slice 08e-3b REVIEW (independent, review-only): the durable merge queue drain wiring into _implement_dag + the post-DAG checkpoint splice (_checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group) that makes the queue LIVE end-to-end, plus the 6-test test_merge_queue_checkpoint.py. ONE P1, TWO P2, one P3. P1: _reconstruct_checkpoint_body (merge_queue.py:1080) writes the queue dag-group:* checkpoint body with results: [] (and no dag_sha256), but the legacy _project_dag_group_checkpoint body (implementation.py:15695-15702) carries the full ImplementationResult dumps; the legacy dag-group:* resume/post-test consumer _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh HARD-REQUIRES len(results)==len(group_task_ids) via _checkpoint_results_match_tasks (implementation.py:18613-18619). Consequences: (1) the _implement_dag resume reconstruction loop (implementation.py:16194-16264) reads the PRESENT dag-group:{g_idx} artifact, runs _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh on the queue body, fails at _checkpoint_results_match_tasks (results: [] vs N>0 tasks), logs 'checkpoint marker is stale' and breaks WITHOUT advancing start_group -- _recover_dag_group_checkpoint_from_proofs is NOT reached (it only runs when the artifact is ABSENT, :16202), refuting the implementer journal divergence-#3 claim of identical resume visibility; (2) the while loop re-enters the completed group, the per-task resume block (implementation.py:16487-16519) sees the dag-task:{tid} marker carrying the pending_durable_merge_queue note and returns workflow_blocked at :16514 -- a fully completed+checkpointed queue group permanently re-blocks the feature; (3) the post-test guard post_test_observation.py:395-433 calls the SAME _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh, every queue checkpoint fails freshness -> stale_groups -> _quiesce_post_test_workflow_blocker(reason=post_test_blocked_dag_checkpoint_stale). Net: a sandbox/queue-driven feature cannot resume past or complete a checkpointed group. Confirmed reachable: project_group_checkpoint -> _insert_or_reuse_artifact -> INSERT INTO artifacts (store.py:3803), the same artifacts table runner.artifacts.get reads. The implementer rated results: [] as a P2-risk pre-existing 08d TODO 'cross-process display only' -- WRONG, a legacy reader indexes results in two load-bearing places (resume + post-test). P2-a (vector 6): on resume after a drain succeeds + the checkpoint crashes, the integrated group is left stranded workflow_blocked -- the per-task resume block re-blocks on the surviving pending marker (:16491-16507) without re-running the idempotent _drain + checkpoint_group, whereas doc 08 Rollback And Recovery Table says integrated/checkpointing groups are recoverable by re-running coverage + the idempotent checkpoint_group; fail-closed, no double-apply (integrated lanes are not re-drained), matches accepted 08e-2 behavior, but doc 08 wants auto-completion -> P2 tracked-08f. P2-b: the 6 tests call _drain_durable_merge_queue_for_feature + _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group as standalone helpers and never exercise the _implement_dag call site, a resume, or the post-test guard over a queue-projected dag-group:* body -- exactly why the P1 shipped undetected. P3: _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group._route uses a fixed item_id=0 for all checkpoint-failure routes (implementation.py:6336) so coverage-not-approved and checkpoint_group-declined share one router idempotency key -- benign (same class, same quiesce route). Vector 7 (gating) VERDICT: correct, no finding -- doc 08 forbids a runtime queue/legacy split and says any flag is a startup readiness guard only; 08e-3b wires NO runtime gate, selects the queue path per-result by the sandbox-stamped pending_durable_merge_queue note, the only guard is the fail-closed verify_merge_queue_production_ready -- doc-conformant; no Slice-12 runtime switch belongs here. Vector 8 (results: []) VERDICT: P1 not P2 -- see P1. Vectors 1/3/5 PASS: call-site step ordering is correct and can reach complete with no group_idx advance on a partially-drained group; the legacy _verify_and_fix_group/_commit_group checkpoint path is byte-for-byte unchanged on the implicit-else branch and the compat shims survive; every failure funnels through typed workflow_blocked + checkpoint_contradiction->quiesce routing with no _commit_group fallback. Vector 4 PARTIAL: the execute() post-DAG verify gates run after _implement_dag returns and are not bypassed/reordered (and now actually run for queue features), but the post_test_observation.py post-test guard, though byte-for-byte untouched, is not preserved in EFFECT -- it shares _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh and actively rejects every queue checkpoint as stale (P1 consequence 3). Verdict: 08e-3b BLOCKED ON P1.

decision: Slice 08e-3b is BLOCKED ON ONE P1: _reconstruct_checkpoint_body writes the queue dag-group:* checkpoint body with results: [], but the legacy dag-group:* freshness gate _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh (via _checkpoint_results_match_tasks) hard-requires len(results)==len(group_task_ids) and that gate is consumed by BOTH the _implement_dag resume reconstruction loop and the post_test_observation.py post-test guard -- so every queue-checkpointed group is judged stale, breaking resume (the completed group re-blocks workflow_blocked or re-runs) AND blocking post-test completion (post_test_blocked_dag_checkpoint_stale quiesce). A sandbox/queue-driven feature cannot complete the develop workflow. The implementer rated this P2-risk 'cross-process display only' -- incorrect, a legacy reader indexes results in two load-bearing places. Two P2s: (a) vector 6 -- on resume after a checkpoint crash an integrated group is stranded workflow_blocked because the per-task resume block re-blocks on the pending marker without re-running the idempotent drain+checkpoint_group; fail-closed and no double-apply, acceptable as a tracked 08f crash-recovery item but doc 08 wants integrated groups auto-completed; (b) the 6 new tests never exercise the _implement_dag call site, a resume, or the post-test guard -- the slice's actual integration surface is untested, which is why the P1 shipped. One P3: the checkpoint _route uses a fixed item_id=0 so two checkpoint-failure causes share a router idempotency key -- benign. The drain+checkpoint call site itself is sound: vectors 1/3/5 PASS (correct step ordering, can reach complete, no group_idx advance on a partial drain, legacy non-queue path byte-for-byte unchanged, every failure fails closed via typed workflow_blocked + checkpoint_contradiction->quiesce with no _commit_group fallback); vector 7 (gating) is doc-conformant with no finding (no runtime gate belongs here -- the per-result pending_durable_merge_queue note + the startup readiness guard are exactly doc 08); vector 4 is PARTIAL -- the execute() post-DAG gates are preserved and now run, but the post-test guard though untouched is not preserved in effect (it rejects the queue checkpoint body). Fix the P1 by populating _reconstruct_checkpoint_body results from typed task-result evidence so the body matches the legacy shape; add the P2-b _implement_dag-level resume + post-test tests; the P2-a resume-recovery wiring is a tracked 08f item. Re-review after the P1 fix.

summary: Slice 08e-3b REMEDIATION of the independent review (ONE P1 BLOCKING + two P2 + one P3): fixed P1 + P2-a + P2-b, deferred P3 to 08g. All additive new code paths; no working 08d/08e-1/08e-2/08e-3a code refactored; the 08d contract unchanged. P1 (FIXED) -- the queue dag-group:* checkpoint body now passes the legacy resume freshness gate _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh (consumed by BOTH the _implement_dag resume reconstruction loop and the post_test_observation.py post-test guard). Investigation found TWO root causes, not one: (1) _reconstruct_checkpoint_body emitted results: [] but _checkpoint_results_match_tasks (implementation.py:18609-18627) hard-requires one validatable ImplementationResult dump per group task id; (2) even with results populated, _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh reads dag-group-commit-proof:* / dag-checkpoint-gate-proof:* artifacts the queue path never writes, falling to _feature_has_execution_control_legacy_marker which is False for a queue/sandbox feature. Part A: GroupMergeCoordinator.__init__ gained an optional additive task_results_provider (default None -- 08d coordinator unit tests still get results: [], no behavior change); _reconstruct_checkpoint_body gained an optional results kwarg; checkpoint_group threads the per-task ImplementationResult model-dumps into the body. _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group gained an additive task_results param and a _task_results_provider emitting exactly one dump per expected task id (defensive fallback synthesizes a minimal completed result carrying only the typed task id -- never fabricated work); the if pending_merge_queue: call site passes task_results=pending_merge_queue. Part B: a NEW helper _dag_group_queue_checkpoint_is_fresh (implementation.py:19383) + a NEW branch in _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh -- when the legacy proof artifacts are absent it checks the durable queue's TYPED merge_queue_items rows back the dag-group:* body (every expected task covered by exactly one done lane, no non-done lane covering an expected task, all done lanes sharing one checkpoint_projection_id, result_commits reconstructing the body commit_hash and matching current repo heads); on success returns True, on any failure (missing module/store, DB error, incomplete evidence) returns False and falls through to the UNCHANGED legacy-marker logic. The durable evidence sources used: the in-memory pending_merge_queue results at the live checkpoint, and the durable dag-task:{tid} attempt markers on resume (reading the per-task result content to reconstruct the body's results list -- doc 08 step-2 'reconstruct the legacy checkpoint body from typed rows'; the queue still never INFERS checkpoint completion from dag-task:*). Confirmed by grep _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh has exactly two real consumers (resume loop implementation.py:16218, post-test guard post_test_observation.py:407); _recover_dag_group_checkpoint_from_proofs only runs when the dag-group:* artifact is absent (not the queue case). P2-a (FIXED) -- a new additive helper _resume_recover_durable_merge_queue_group (implementation.py:6521) + _MergeQueueResumeRecovery re-drives the idempotent _drain_durable_merge_queue_for_feature (no-op for already-integrated lanes -- claim cannot re-take them, no double-apply) + _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group (idempotent no-op for an already-checkpointed group); a new additive block in _implement_dag's per-task resume area probes for any pending dag-task-pending-merge:* marker and on recovered=True records handover, deletes the stale markers, logs, refreshes the exhibit, group_idx += 1; continue; on recovered=False falls through to the UNCHANGED fail-closed per-task pending-marker workflow_blocked block. Never falls back to the legacy _commit_group. Surgical: gated entirely on a pending marker; placed at the reviewed per-task resume block, not earlier in the loop. P2-b (FIXED) -- 7 new real-Postgres tests in test_merge_queue_checkpoint.py (6 original -> 13 total): the freshness gate over a real queue-projected dag-group:* body, a pre-P1-fix-shape rejection pin, the REAL _implement_dag resume reconstruction loop reaching complete without dispatch, the post-test guard freshness loop, and the P2-a recovery (drained-uncheckpointed completion, idempotent re-run no double-projection, fail-closed on a failed lane). The 4 P1 freshness/resume/post-test tests were run against a pre-fix simulation (results: [] reverted + Part B branch disabled) -- ALL 4 FAILED, including the _implement_dag resume test which failed assert 'workflow_blocked' == 'complete'; source restored, all 13 pass. P3 (DEFERRED to 08g) -- the _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group._route fixed item_id=0 shares a router idempotency key between two checkpoint-failure causes; benign (same failure_class, same deterministic quiesce route); a fix needs either a signature change to accepted 08e-3a _route_merge_queue_drain_failure (no-refactor non-negotiable) or a fake non-zero item_id that misrepresents merge_queue_item_id in the payload -- deferred as a tracked 08g to-do.

decision: Fix the P1 in TWO parts because it has two root causes: (A) populate the queue dag-group:* body's results from the per-task ImplementationResult evidence (in-memory pending_merge_queue at the live checkpoint; the durable dag-task:{tid} attempt markers on resume) via an additive optional task_results_provider on GroupMergeCoordinator -- the 08d contract is unchanged; (B) add a new additive branch + helper _dag_group_queue_checkpoint_is_fresh to _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh that recognizes a queue-projected checkpoint by its typed done merge_queue_items lanes when the legacy dag-group-commit-proof:* / dag-checkpoint-gate-proof:* artifacts are absent, failing closed to the unchanged legacy-marker logic. Fix P2-a now (not deferred) with an additive _resume_recover_durable_merge_queue_group helper that re-drives the idempotent drain + checkpoint from the per-task resume block when a pending marker is present, falling through to the unchanged fail-closed workflow_blocked block on failure. Fix P2-b with 7 new real-Postgres tests including the real _implement_dag resume call site and the post-test guard. Defer P3 to 08g.
alternatives_rejected: P1 Part A: change the 08d GroupMergeCoordinator.checkpoint_group(coverage, token) signature to take the results -- rejected: the remediation prompt + no-refactor non-negotiable say prefer reconstructing from existing evidence over an 08d-contract change; an OPTIONAL additive task_results_provider on __init__ (default None) achieves it with zero 08d behavior change.

P1 Part A: reconstruct results from a latest-artifact scan of dag-task:* -- rejected: doc 08 forbids the queue inferring checkpoint completion from dag-task:*; the live path uses the in-memory pending_merge_queue and resume uses the dag-task:* marker CONTENT only to rebuild the body's results list (doc-08 step-2 reconstruct-from-typed-rows), never to decide completion.

P1 Part B: make the queue checkpoint also write the legacy dag-group-commit-proof:* and dag-checkpoint-gate-proof:* artifacts so the existing freshness gate passes unchanged -- rejected: faithfully reconstructing those artifacts requires re-running the entire legacy _record_dag_checkpoint_gate_proof machinery (merge_gate/checkpoint_gate typed nodes with stage=checkpoint, dag-verify graph projections, no-dirty digests) -- a large duplication; the queue's typed done merge_queue_items rows are a STRONGER proof, and recognizing them in an additive new branch is the minimal correct fix.

P1 Part B: relax _checkpoint_results_match_tasks or the proof-artifact requirement directly -- rejected: that would weaken the legacy resume gate for the legacy path; the new branch is purely additive and the legacy path is byte-for-byte unchanged.

P2-a: defer the resume re-drive to 08f as the 08e-3b implementer's journal proposed -- rejected: loop discipline forbids progressing with an open P2; after the P1 fix both the drain and checkpoint_group are idempotent so the re-drive is safe (no double-apply -- claim cannot re-take integrated lanes), and the remediation prompt's strong default is FIX IT NOW; the fix is surgical (gated on a pending marker, at the reviewed per-task resume block).

P2-a: place the re-drive earlier in the resume reconstruction loop (before _compile_task_contracts_for_group / _ensure_task_worktrees) -- rejected: that is the broad resume-loop surgery the remediation prompt warns against; the reviewed problem is the per-task resume block re-blocking, and the fix is placed exactly there, mirroring the main if pending_merge_queue: block which is also after that setup.

P2-b: drive a full _implement_dag end-to-end for the P2-a recovery -- rejected: the resume path runs _compile_task_contracts_for_group which needs a real dag artifact + workspace registry fixture before the per-task resume block is reached; testing _resume_recover_durable_merge_queue_group directly (real Postgres) is the faithful unambiguous coverage, and test_implement_dag_resume_skips_a_queue_checkpointed_group already drives the real _implement_dag resume call site for the P1.

P3: fix the fixed item_id=0 router key now -- rejected: a clean fix needs a signature change to accepted 08e-3a _route_merge_queue_drain_failure (no-refactor non-negotiable) or a fake non-zero item_id that misrepresents merge_queue_item_id/queue_item_id in the failure payload; the review rated it benign (same failure_class, same deterministic quiesce route) and optional -- deferred to 08g as a tracked to-do.

summary: Slice 08e-3b RE-REVIEW (independent, re-review-only) of the P1+P2-a+P2-b remediation. Verdict: STILL BLOCKED — one P1 remains. The remediation correctly fixed the empty-results half of the original P1 (the body now carries one ImplementationResult dump per task id via the additive task_results_provider) and added a sound typed-merge_queue_items-evidence freshness branch (_dag_group_queue_checkpoint_is_fresh, implementation.py:19383) — and the shared-consumer legacy non-queue path is byte-for-byte UNCHANGED (vector 2 CLEAN: the new branch in _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh activates only when both legacy proof artifacts are absent AND merge_queue_items rows exist; a legacy feature always writes dag-group-commit-proof:* so it never enters the branch, and _dag_group_queue_checkpoint_is_fresh returns False for any feature with no queue rows). P2-a (_resume_recover_durable_merge_queue_group) and P2-b (7 honest tests) are correctly remediated; fail-closed holds with no _commit_group fallback on any durable path. BUT the P1 fix is INCOMPLETE: the queue dag-group:* body builds task_ids from coverage.expected_task_ids, which coverage() sets to sorted(expected) (merge_queue.py:868) and _reconstruct_checkpoint_body copies verbatim (merge_queue.py:1116); _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group also sorts (ordered_expected=sorted, implementation.py:6334). So the projected body task_ids is lexically SORTED. The shared legacy resume freshness gate _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh does an ORDER-SENSITIVE list(checkpoint[task_ids]) != list(group_task_ids) compare at implementation.py:19521, evaluated BEFORE the new typed-evidence branch; its callers pass DAG/wave order (resume loop: list(dag.execution_order[g]), implementation.py:16408; post-test guard: post_test_observation.py:395/411). dag.execution_order group order is _sort_key order (task_planning.py:3278/3338) dominated by task_order, NOT lexical task-id order; the legacy _verify_and_fix_group body uses DAG order ([t.id for t in group_tasks], implementation.py:15910) so the legacy path matches but the queue path does not. For any non-lexically-sorted DAG group the queue checkpoint is rejected as stale — the ORIGINAL P1 symptom recurs (resume break + post-test stale-quiesce). The 13 tests miss it: every test group is trivially sorted ([TASK-1], [TASK-A,TASK-B]). Fix: thread the DAG/wave order into the body task_ids (not a sorted copy); add a non-lexically-sorted multi-task group test driving the real _implement_dag resume + the post-test guard.

summary: Slice 08e-3b REMEDIATION 2 of the independent RE-REVIEW's single residual P1: the queue dag-group:* checkpoint body's task_ids was lexically SORTED while the shared legacy resume freshness gate _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh compares it ORDER-SENSITIVELY against the DAG/wave task order, so a non-lexically-sorted queue-checkpointed group was still rejected as stale (the ORIGINAL P1 symptom: resume break without advancing start_group; post-test guard stale-quiesce). Narrow additive ordering fix; no working 08d/08e-1/08e-2/08e-3a/first-08e-3b code refactored, the 08d contract unchanged. Root cause: _dag_group_checkpoint_is_fresh at implementation.py:19521 does list(checkpoint[task_ids]) != list(group_task_ids) (element-wise ordered) in the SHARED part of the function before the new typed-evidence branch; its callers pass DAG/wave order (resume loop group_task_ids=list(dag.execution_order[g_idx]) implementation.py:16408; post-test guard enumerate(dag.execution_order) post_test_observation.py:395/411); dag.execution_order group order is _sort_key order dominated by task_order (task_planning.py:3248-3253), NOT lexical; the legacy _verify_and_fix_group body uses DAG order ([t.id for t in group_tasks] implementation.py:15910) so the legacy path matched but the queue path did not -- the queue body built task_ids from coverage.expected_task_ids which coverage() sets to sorted(expected) (merge_queue.py:868), copied verbatim by _reconstruct_checkpoint_body (merge_queue.py:1116), and _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group re-sorted again (ordered_expected=sorted, implementation.py:6334). Fix in three additive layers mirroring how the first remediation threaded results: (1) merge_queue.py -- _reconstruct_checkpoint_body gained an optional task_ids kwarg (None falls back to coverage.expected_task_ids, 08d unit-test behavior unchanged); a new BodyTaskIdsProvider type and an optional 5th GroupMergeCoordinator.__init__ positional body_task_ids_provider (default None); checkpoint_group calls it when wired and passes the result as _reconstruct_checkpoint_body(task_ids=body_task_ids). (2) implementation.py -- _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group gained an additive dag_ordered_task_ids parameter; it filters that list to the expected task-id SET preserving DAG order and uses it only when it fully covers the expected set, else falls back to the sorted ordered_expected (fail-soft to the pre-remediation body shape, never a legacy commit); a new _body_task_ids_provider closure is passed as the coordinator's 5th argument. (3) the two call sites thread the DAG order: the if pending_merge_queue: block passes dag_ordered_task_ids=list(group) (group = dag.execution_order[group_idx], the SAME wave-ordered list the resume gate / post-test guard pass as group_task_ids); the P2-a resume recovery _resume_recover_durable_merge_queue_group passes dag_ordered_task_ids=list(group_task_ids) (its param is the caller's list(group)). P2-A (doc-08-conformant crashed-checkpointing recovery): a crashed checkpointing lane is recovered by RE-RUNNING the idempotent group checkpoint (doc 08 Rollback And Recovery Table line 655), never by a repo reset (a checkpointing lane is past integrated -- already committed + proved clean -- so a reset would destroy a real canonical commit). (1) merge_queue_store.py:76 _LEASE_TRANSITIONS gained the checkpointing:{failed,poisoned} key (doc-341/342 active-non-terminal->failed/poisoned; schema-CHECK-legal -- failed/poisoned are in no merge_queue_items proof-progression NOT IN list); checkpointing->done stays coordinator-owned via complete_checkpoint, deliberately not in this table -- this alone closes the silent loop. (2) implementation.py _recover_one_crashed_merge_queue_lane gained a status==checkpointing branch -> new _recover_one_crashed_checkpointing_group, which reconstructs the group expected task ids from EVERY lane merge_queue_task_coverage row (the 08e-2 enqueue produces one task:{id} lane per task), reconstructs per-task ImplementationResults from the durable dag-task:* markers when runner.artifacts is present, and re-drives the idempotent _checkpoint_durable_merge_queue_group; on completion the lane converges to done, else the new _poison_unrecoverable_checkpointing_lane drives it to terminal poisoned (the doc-655 other terminal). (3) _MergeQueueDrainResult gained an additive succeeded property (terminal_status in integrated/done) used by the drain-loop logging and the two failed_lanes filters so a recovered-done lane is not mistaken for a failure. P2-A surfaced a REAL latent bug in the reviewed 08d GroupMergeCoordinator.checkpoint_group: its covered set (and _checkpoint_coverage_digest) was integrated UNION done -- a checkpointing lane was in NEITHER, so complete_checkpoint([]) raised and a re-driven checkpoint of an all-checkpointing group could never succeed (violating doc 08 Tests Repeating checkpoint from checkpointing rows succeeds); fixed additively -- GroupMergeCoverage gained a checkpointing_queue_item_ids field (default_factory=list, backward-compatible), coverage() populates it, and checkpoint_group covered + _checkpoint_coverage_digest + the already-checkpointed short-circuit guard all include checkpointing lanes (the digest is now status-INVARIANT). decision: Slice 09c SPLIT into 09c-1 (activation + rollback -- this entry, fully verified) and 09c-2 (the RegroupOverlayResolver + the implementation.py dispatch-resolution swap -- LEFT). The 3 09b-2 reviewer-watch items honored: (1) a corrected overlay re-validating to a different validation_digest makes record_validation raise RegroupOverlayValidationConflict -- the internal _ConflictDetected sentinel rolls the activation transaction back CLEANLY, activate_overlay then re-stages a fresh overlay (a NEW deterministically-salted overlay_id) OUTSIDE the rolled-back transaction so the fresh row durably commits, and raises OverlayConflictNeedsFreshOverlay -- the error is surfaced, not swallowed; (2) P3-9 -- the activation transaction builds the RegroupActiveMarker FROM the just-written canonical/rollback artifact ids + the freshly-computed canonical body sha, performing the canonical-artifact-body-sha + projection-link-id checks the validator step 12 deferred; (3) activation READS latest_successful_validation_id / validation_digest from the overlay row after the re-validation (record_validation owns advancing them) -- never re-derived. The activation module is a sibling leaf module (it must call both RegroupOverlayStore and validate_overlay, and regroup_overlay_store imports regroup_overlay -- regroup_overlay.py would be a circular import; the 09a no-refactor discipline forbids editing the model module). doc-09-ambiguity resolutions: activation re-runs validate_overlay first (the only ordering that both honors reviewer-watch item 3 and admits the conflict/re-stage path -- a never-validated structurally-valid overlay IS activatable, a structurally-invalid one is rejected); the forbidden group-scoped artifact prefix set is the gN-spelled dag-<kind>:g{i}: family verified against implementation.py; rollback reads compatibility_artifact_ids from the row column (the typed model has no such field).
alternatives_rejected: do 09c in one chunk (activation + rollback + resolver + the implementation.py dispatch swap -- too large; STATUS.md + the loop-discipline SPLIT rule flag it; the resolver consumes what activation writes and the dispatch swap is a 33k-line monolith edit)
put activate_overlay/rollback_overlay in regroup_overlay.py (circular import vs regroup_overlay_store; violates the 09a no-refactor discipline)

re-stage the fresh overlay inside the activation transaction (it would be undone by the conflict rollback -- a real bug; the conflict path is deliberately split out of _activate_overlay_txn so the re-stage commits durably)
read latest_successful_validation_id BEFORE re-validating (rejects a never-validated structurally-valid overlay and cannot reach the conflict/re-stage path; re-validate-first is the correct ordering)

summary: Slice 09c-2 IMPLEMENTATION -- the RegroupOverlayResolver + the implementation.py dispatch-resolution swap. Delivered as ONE cohesive sub-deliverable (NOT split): the swap is small/surgical (the legacy G45-G73 function body is renamed UNCHANGED, the new wrapper preserves the exact signature + return tuple so the 3 _implement_dag call sites need no edit) and the resolver lives in its own new leaf module. (A) New leaf module workflows/develop/execution/regroup_overlay_resolver.py -- RegroupOverlayResolver (connection-bound, holds a RegroupOverlayStore over the caller conn) + the RegroupOverlayResolution result model. RegroupOverlayResolver.resolve(feature_id, group_idx) is a pure READ path (no writes, no advisory lock): it reads typed overlay state FIRST (RegroupOverlayStore.get_active_overlay) and returns one of three mutually-exclusive shapes -- no-typed-overlay (has_typed_overlay=False, the caller keeps its non-overlay behavior), applied (effective_execution_order = base prefix waves [0..offset-1] + the overlay derived suffix waves), or quiesce (a regroup_invalid* diagnostic, dispatch fails closed). FAIL-CLOSED is the core property: every path where a typed overlay EXISTS but does not pass every check produces a quiesce, NEVER an executable effective_execution_order; a stale/orphaned compatibility marker can only ever yield a diagnostic regroup_invalid quiesce. The resolver loads the active typed row, the row projection-link columns (active_marker_projection_id / compatibility_artifact_ids / latest_successful_validation_id / validation_digest -- the typed RegroupOverlay model has no counterpart fields), the latest dag-regroup-active:{slug} marker, the source DAG by id+hash, and the latest canonical dag-regroup:{slug} projection; it applies the overlay ONLY when all ids/hashes/status/digest/projection-links match. Per the carried P3-A the resolver does the FULL marker id/sha cross-check 09c-1 rollback _cross_check_marker deferred: marker.canonical_artifact_id == the loaded canonical row id AND in compatibility_artifact_ids; marker.canonical_sha256 == sha256 of the loaded canonical artifact body; marker.rollback_artifact_id and the row active_marker_projection_id in compatibility_artifact_ids; marker.overlay_row_id == the typed row id. It funnels through validate_overlay(activation_check=True, persist=False) so the validator step-12 RegroupActiveMarker field checks run (and steps 1-11 re-prove dependency/write-set/barrier/mapping preservation against the freshly-loaded base DAG); it also re-derives the canonical projection via build_canonical_projection (the SAME 09c-1 function that WROTE it) and requires the re-derived body sha to equal the stored canonical artifact body sha (canonical-drift detection). The effective execution order is base_dag.execution_order[:offset] + the derived suffix; the root dag is never overwritten. (B) The dispatch-resolution swap in implementation.py: the legacy G45-G73 _resolve_active_regroup_before_group_dispatch body is RENAMED to _resolve_active_regroup_legacy_marker BYTE-FOR-BYTE UNCHANGED; _resolve_active_regroup_before_group_dispatch is now a thin wrapper with the SAME signature + return shape (ImplementationDAG|None, str, dict) -- it runs the typed _resolve_active_regroup_typed_overlay first (when an active typed overlay row exists the resolver is authoritative: apply OR fail-closed quiesce), and when NO typed overlay row exists falls through to the UNCHANGED legacy marker path (doc 09 SS Refactoring Steps 2 -- the legacy projection aliases stay readable for the atomic landing). Non-overlay behavior is preserved EXACTLY: a feature with no active overlay (no typed row AND no legacy marker) dispatches exactly as before. dag_regroup.py UNTOUCHED. 25 real-Postgres resolver tests pass; the 68-test legacy regroup compatibility suite (test_dag_regroup.py 27 + test_regroup_overlay.py 41) STAYS GREEN; the 570-test execution suite and 124 store/validator/activation tests and 302 quiesce/expanded-verify tests all pass.

summary: Slice 09d-2 IMPLEMENTATION -- the conservative cap computation + the typed SchedulerFeedback + the review:dag-sizing:{feature_id}:{window} projection. The LAST Slice 09 implementation sub-slice (09e is the slice-end review); it consumes 09d-1 build_scheduler_group_metrics output. The cap-computation vs feedback-emission was NOT split further -- they are tightly coupled (the SchedulerFeedback IS the typed carrier of the cap output) and the whole thing is a bounded formula-transcription + emission chunk done in one verified pass. NEW sibling leaf module workflows/develop/execution/scheduler_sizing.py (NOT scheduler_metrics.py -- 09d-1 is review-CLEAN and the no-refactor discipline keeps it untouched; NOT regroup_overlay.py -- the 09a model-only module; the same sibling-leaf pattern as 09b-2/09c-1/09c-2/09d-1). (A) The conservative cap computation, every formula VERBATIM from doc 09 SS Scheduler-Metrics-And-Cap-Rules (== SS Adaptive-Sizing-Data-Flow steps 5-6): compute_policy_cap -- doc-09 step 1 four tiers VERBATIM (unknown writes or high-risk barriers -> 4 / backend or multi-repo -> 6 / isolated UI-document -> 10 / test-only and perf -> 14), evaluated most-conservative-first, an UNCLASSIFIED lane/barrier falls conservatively to the 4-cap. compute_evidence_cap -- doc-09 step 4 VERBATIM evidence_cap = floor(12h_checkpoint_budget / hours_per_task_p75) clamped to [4, policy_cap]; CHECKPOINT_BUDGET_HOURS=12.0; None when no p75. compute_cap_decision -- the reduction triggers doc-09 steps 2/3/6: step 2 MIN_SAMPLES_TO_WIDEN=2 (a candidate strictly above current_cap with <2 completed samples WITH evidence ids is clamped to current_cap); step 6 stale data likewise clamps the widen; step 3 RATE_REGRESSION_THRESHOLD=0.10 -- a window per-task rate > baseline*1.10 reduces to min(current_cap, 4); the result is never below MIN_CAP=4. _GlobalBaseline -- doc-09 step 4 the global post-change baseline, built from ALL completed metrics. build_candidate_waves/_can_join_wave -- doc-09 step 5 the post-cap dependency/hard-barrier/write-set/mapping validators run AFTER cap selection and may SHRINK or REJECT a candidate wave (cap is the upper bound only): hard-barrier mix (step 9), same-wave write-set overlap after path canonicalization (step 10), an unknown_write or missing-write-set-contract task scheduled alone (step 7), incomplete merged-group write-set coverage (step 7); an unsatisfiable remaining graph fails fast and surfaces no waves rather than crash. (B) compute_scheduler_feedback is the PURE computation (no DB/clock/write) returning an AdaptiveSizingResult carrying the typed SchedulerFeedback (the 09a model, every field) + the CapDecision + the CandidateWave list; the completed-throughput aggregates (hours_per_task p50/p75 nearest-rank, tasks_per_hour, queue_wait_p75_h, the four per-task rates, verify_cost_per_task) are computed from COMPLETED groups ONLY (doc-09 step 2); lane/barrier aggregation by barrier:{barrier} when >=2 completed samples else lane:{lane} else policy (doc-09 step 4). feedback_id = _derive_feedback_id sha256 over sorted-keys (feature_id, window_start/end, lane, barrier, overlay_id or root, sorted metric_ids) -- generated_at DELIBERATELY EXCLUDED so a re-run is idempotent. persist_scheduler_feedback writes EXACTLY ONE execution_scheduler_feedback row via the 09b RegroupOverlayStore.insert_scheduler_feedback (idempotent). project_sizing_review writes ONE review:dag-sizing:{feature_id}:{window} artifacts row ({window}=g{start}-g{end}) with an explicit executable:false/is_active_marker:false stamp (idempotent on identical body). run_adaptive_sizing is the end-to-end flow (09d-1 metric build read-path -> compute_scheduler_feedback pure -> persist + project); persist=False writes NOTHING. CRITICAL SAFETY -- 09d-2 NEVER writes an active marker: its ENTIRE persistence surface is exactly TWO writes (one execution_scheduler_feedback row + one review:dag-sizing:* artifact) and only when persist=True; persist_scheduler_feedback calls ONLY insert_scheduler_feedback (which inserts only into execution_scheduler_feedback), project_sizing_review runs ONE INSERT INTO artifacts with a key ALWAYS review:dag-sizing:* (never dag-regroup-active:*/dag-regroup:*/dag-regroup-rollback:*), run_adaptive_sizing never imports/calls activate_overlay and never inserts an execution_regroup_overlays/events row; the review:dag-sizing:* key is NEVER consumed by RegroupOverlayResolver; the cap recommendation can affect dispatch ONLY by being converted into a new RegroupOverlay and passing the full 13-step validate_overlay + activate_overlay. data_quality degradation -- _resolve_data_quality reads 09d-1 data_quality_flags off the contributing metrics: any missing_projection_lineage -> stale (doc-09 step 3); else any flag with EVERY metric flagged -> insufficient, some -> mixed; no flag -> sufficient; a stale window also BLOCKS the widen. dag_regroup.py is UNTOUCHED.

decision: Slice 09d-2 delivered as a NEW sibling leaf module scheduler_sizing.py. The cap-computation vs feedback-emission was kept as ONE verified sub-slice (no further split): they are tightly coupled (the SchedulerFeedback IS the typed carrier of the cap output) and the whole is a bounded formula-transcription + emission chunk. doc-09 ambiguity resolutions: (1) the four policy_cap tiers name no concrete barrier ids / lane names -- classify the free-text OverlayTaskSpeedMetadata barrier/semantic_lane by documented substring markers, most-conservative tier first, an UNRECOGNIZED lane falls to the 4-cap (sound -- doc 09 says caps are conservative; a mis-tag can only shrink, never widen unsafely). (2) no feedback_id formula -- mint it sha256-over-sorted-keys in the 09a derive_overlay_id/derive_metric_id style, EXCLUDING generated_at so a re-run over the same metric set is idempotent. (3) no confidence formula -- low when data not sufficient or <2 samples, high when sufficient + >=4 samples, medium otherwise. (4) the percentile estimator -- nearest-rank (no interpolation), the simplest fully deterministic choice. (5) doc-09 step 3 reduce to current cap OR 4 -- min(current_cap, 4), the more conservative reading. (6) doc-09 step 6 never widen from a window omitting typed merge/checkpoint proof -- by 09d-1 construction a completed=True metric ALWAYS has the full proof, so the remaining staleness trigger is the projection-lineage signal (stale driven by data_quality==stale). (7) current_cap is a caller-supplied parameter -- the caller owns the cap currently in force; 09d-2 does not infer a legacy cap source and leaves dag_regroup.py recommend_adaptive_sizing untouched. The NEVER-writes-an-active-marker safety property is structural: the entire persistence surface is two writes (one execution_scheduler_feedback row via the 09b store, one review:dag-sizing:* artifact); 09d-2 never imports activate_overlay, never writes an overlay/events/dag-regroup-active row; the review key is never consumed by RegroupOverlayResolver; the cap recommendation reaches dispatch ONLY via a new overlay + the full validate_overlay + activate_overlay. The carried P3-7/P3-8/P3-D confirmed: 09d-2 writes no dag-regroup-validation:* artifact so no key collision (P3-7); it reads the overlay typed speed_index/barriers/write_sets + the optional Slice 3 write_sets param and does NOT assume the validator enforced live Slice 3 contracts -- the candidate-wave validators are 09d-2 own recommendation-time checks not a validate_overlay substitute (P3-8); it is offset-agnostic (P3-D). 09d-1 P3-E/P3-G noted: 09d-2 verify_cost_per_task reads 09d-1 verify_cost_units (the typed verification-cost sum, NOT the overlapping verify_count) so the P3-E overlap does not skew the cap; the P3-G merge_conflicts upward bias only SHRINKS the 09d-2 cap (conservative-safe).
alternatives_rejected: Split 09d-2 again into the cap computation vs the feedback emission -- rejected: they are tightly coupled (the SchedulerFeedback IS the typed carrier of the cap decision); splitting would force a half-built feedback model across two iterations; the whole is a bounded formula-transcription + emission chunk that verifies cleanly in one pass.

Put the cap computation in scheduler_metrics.py (09d-1 module) -- rejected: 09d-1 is review-CLEAN and the 09a/09b/09c/09d-1 no-refactor discipline keeps it untouched; the same reason 09b-2/09c-1/09c-2 are sibling leaf modules.
Put it in regroup_overlay.py (doc 09 SS Proposed-Interfaces says that module owns scheduler feedback) -- rejected: regroup_overlay.py is the 09a model-only module the no-refactor discipline protects.

Have 09d-2 convert its own recommendation into an active overlay / write an active marker -- rejected and FORBIDDEN: doc 09 SS Validation-Algorithm + SS Adaptive-Sizing-Data-Flow step 8 are explicit -- metrics can only produce a recommendation artifact; the recommendation must be converted into a NEW RegroupOverlay and pass the full 13-step validate_overlay before activation; 09d-2 emits ONLY the SchedulerFeedback row + the review:dag-sizing:* projection.

Reuse dag_regroup.recommend_adaptive_sizing (the legacy recommender) -- rejected: doc 09 SS Refactoring-Steps item 6 makes the typed SchedulerFeedback + review:dag-sizing:* projection the new output; the legacy path stays a compatibility source until 09e the facade conversion; dag_regroup.py is left UNTOUCHED.

Infer current_cap from a legacy artifact / the prior overlay -- rejected: doc 09 carries current_cap as a model field; the caller (09e the facade conversion / the future scheduler wiring) owns supplying the cap currently in force; 09d-2 takes it as a parameter and makes no legacy-cap-source assumption.

Use an interpolating percentile estimator for p50/p75 -- rejected: doc 09 pins no estimator; nearest-rank is the simplest fully deterministic choice and never invents an intermediate value (conservative-by-construction).

summary: Slice 10a -- the typed ControlPlaneSnapshot store + models (doc 10 Refactoring Steps steps 1-2, the FIRST split seam of the large Slice 10). NEW snapshots.py carries the typed ControlPlaneSnapshot Pydantic contract + 13 summary models (SnapshotBudget, ControlPlaneSnapshotQuery, SnapshotCursor, EvidenceRef, ExecutionAttemptSummary, WorkspaceSnapshotSummary, TypedFailureSummary, MergeQueueSummary, RetryBudgetSummary, GateStatusSummary, SandboxLeaseSummary, RuntimeBindingSummary, SupervisorDigest) with field lists transcribed verbatim from doc 10 Proposed Interfaces/Types, plus control_plane_snapshot_version (a deterministic order-independent digest over sorted (table,max_id,max_updated_at) cursor triples). ADDITIVE to store.py: the typed snapshot builder layer (build_typed_control_plane_snapshot, compute_typed_snapshot_version, _typed_snapshot_version_cursors, helpers) + two new ExecutionControlStore methods get_control_plane_snapshot / get_control_plane_snapshot_version + the _set_local_statement_timeout staticmethod. The pre-Slice-10 dict-based fetch_control_plane_snapshot is left UNTOUCHED (later sub-slices switch consumers). EVERY snapshot read is bounded: 8 list reads each ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT cap+1 with the +1 sentinel detected then dropped for explicit truncation (omitted_counts records >=1 omitted; SnapshotCursor carries keyset position); a SET LOCAL statement_timeout at the store-transaction boundary (SET LOCAL so it cannot leak onto a pooled connection); WHERE feature_id=$1 + optional AND group_idx=$N applied before any aggregate; SUMMARY-ONLY columns -- workspace dirty/forbidden path lists reduced to jsonb_array_length count + first-N sample IN SQL so the full list never crosses the boundary, summary columns substring(...1..500), no value/content/payload-body/prompt/stdout-stderr column ever selected. On a query cancel _typed_snapshot_fetch degrades that section to [] + a degradation_reasons entry, never an unbounded retry. get_control_plane_snapshot_version digests 8 cheap feature-scoped MAX(id)/MAX(updated_at) aggregates -- never an artifact body; a budget-only/sandbox-only update advances it via its own cursor. Slice 10a was not further split (models+methods are small and coupled).

summary: Slice 10c-1 IMPLEMENTATION -- the mechanical read-only supervisor contract. Slice 10c was SPLIT (STATUS.md flagged this): 10c-1 (this entry) = the read-only mechanical enforcement, delivered + fully verified; 10c-2 (deferred) = the typed SupervisorDigest classifier mapping (typed-primary / legacy-fallback gating, the 27-row coverage table for every Slice-07 FailureClass + the static coverage test, the deterministic-class-never-escalates rule) AND the real typed budget source resolving carried P3-10a-1. Split rationale: classifier.py is 2433 lines on the LIVE failure-routing path and the typed mapping is a genuine behavior change; 10c-1 is a self-contained mechanically-verifiable SAFETY contract -- a smaller fully-verified chunk over a large shaky one (loop discipline). WHAT WAS BUILT: a NEW leaf module src/iriai_build_v2/supervisor/read_only.py (additive, imports only typing) + three wiring edits. (1) The execution-authority writer surface, split + complete: CONTROL_PLANE_WRITER_METHODS (28 names exclusive to ExecutionControlStore -- typed journal rows + attempt state transitions, typed failures/route decisions, verification-graph gate evidence, execution-artifact projection writes/checkpoints, task contracts, workspace authority/snapshots, sandbox leases/runtime bindings -- each a doc-10 mutation class); FEATURE_TIMELINE_WRITER_METHODS (7 dual-use feature/artifact-store writers create/transition_phase/update_metadata/log_event/put/write_artifact_bytes/delete, tracked separately because the store CLASS legitimately carries them and the MCP service needs the same class read methods); EXECUTION_AUTHORITY_WRITER_METHODS = the union (35). (2) The mechanical fail-closed construction guard: assert_no_control_plane_writer(handle, role) inspects the handle ITSELF (not a runtime mode flag) and raises ReadOnlySupervisorViolation if it exposes any CONTROL_PLANE_WRITER_METHODS member (those writers are exclusive to ExecutionControlStore, so a handle exposing one IS that store); assert_read_only_supervisor_handles is the single service-construction guard with a named execution_control_store slot that must be None (proving the writer store is STRUCTURALLY ABSENT). (3) The mechanically fail-closed read-only path: SupervisorEvidenceMcpService.__init__ now calls self.assert_read_only() EAGERLY for caller-supplied stores and _ensure_stores() RE-asserts after lazy PostgresFeatureStore/PostgresArtifactStore creation -- the MCP service has no ExecutionControlStore slot so the execution-authority writer is structurally absent; wiring it with an ExecutionControlStore in either store slot raises at construction BEFORE any tool call / runtime parameter is inspected. ActionPolicy.__init__ gained an execution_authority param (default None) + calls assert_no_control_plane_writer on it -- a control-plane writer is NEVER valid on an ActionPolicy in read-only OR guarded mode (doc 10: guarded actions still cannot mutate typed execution state). maybe_restart/maybe_kill_stale_codex are byte-for-byte UNTOUCHED. (4) The fail-closed denied-write path + blocked-action audit row: ActionPolicy.guard_execution_write(packet, action, reason) is a NEW method -- the fail-closed handler that NEVER mutates, records a SupervisorActionStatus.BLOCKED SupervisorActionRecord via the existing _write (action_key(...):blocked -> supervisor-action:* supervisor-owned audit key) and returns it (doc 10: denied writes fail closed and produce a blocked action audit row rather than a best-effort mutation). ReadOnlyAuditArtifactSink(artifact_store) is a mechanical ArtifactSink wrapper: put to a SUPERVISOR_OWNED_AUDIT_KEY_PREFIXES key (supervisor-observation:/decision:/agent-assessment:/thread-context:/action:, + pre-registered supervisor-digest:/digest-audit:/digest-state: for the later Slack-dedupe sub-slice) passes through; put to ANY other key raises BlockedExecutionWrite and performs NO mutation (verified: the wrapped store puts list is empty after a denied put). HOW THE READ-ONLY PATH IS MECHANICALLY FAIL-CLOSED: the deny is STRUCTURAL not a runtime self.mode check -- the MCP service has no ExecutionControlStore slot (structurally absent, asserted at construction + after lazy creation); ActionPolicy has no execution-authority writer field that is ever non-None for a correct wiring; ReadOnlyAuditArtifactSink denies by RAISING so the wrapped store is never reached. DOC-10 AMBIGUITY: doc 10 says the supervisor receives only read/query handles + audit writers, but the MCP service genuinely holds PostgresFeatureStore/PostgresArtifactStore which are full read/WRITE store classes -- a naive no-writer-methods assertion would wrongly reject the read surface. RESOLVED: split the mutation surface -- the execution-authority writers doc 10 enumerates live ENTIRELY on ExecutionControlStore (CONTROL_PLANE_WRITER_METHODS, the construction guard asserts no held handle IS that store); the dual-use feature/artifact writers are enforced at the CALL site (the MCP service exposes no write tool -- verified mechanically; the artifact put is denied by ReadOnlyAuditArtifactSink).

summary: Slice 10d-1 IMPLEMENTATION -- the supervisor Slack dedupe store + the two supervisor-owned tables (doc 10 Slack Dedupe And Suppression, step 7). Slice 10d was SPLIT (STATUS.md flagged it): the store/schema vs. the SupervisorSlackService send/suppress routing are separable seams; 10d-1 delivers the store + tables + decision logic, 10d-2 (next) wires it into watch_and_digest. NEW: schema.sql gains supervisor_digest_state (13 cols, the latest aggregate per dedupe key, unique (feature_id,dedupe_key) + idx_supervisor_dedupe_state_updated/_group) and supervisor_digest_audit (14 cols, append-only send/suppress decisions, idx_supervisor_dedupe_audit_feature/_key/_group) -- both transcribed VERBATIM from the doc-10 column contracts; DISTINCT from the legacy supervisor_slack_digest_* tables (additive, not a rename). They are AUDIT state not execution authority (no artifacts row for dedupe state). supervisor/models.py gains SupervisorDigestKey + SupervisorDigestDecision (shapes + the 6-value reason Literal transcribed verbatim from doc 10). NEW additive leaf supervisor/digest_dedupe.py: SupervisorDigestDedupeStore -- compute_dedupe_key (stable sha256 over the key with the 3 list fields SORTED so list-ordering churn never invents a key), decide() (the doc-10 send/suppress procedure: never-suppress exceptions; first_seen; suppressed_duplicate same-snapshot idempotency; suppressed_within_cooldown 30-min cooldown + coalesce; post-cooldown coalesced/material_change send), record_sent/record_suppressed (ON CONFLICT (feature_id,dedupe_key) upsert + append-only audit row; record_sent resets suppressed_count, record_suppressed COALESCEs last_sent_at so a suppression never looks like a send), bounded get_state/audit_history. The 30-min cooldown is a module constant SUPPRESSION_COOLDOWN_SECONDS not a caller preference. The never-suppress exceptions (CORRECTNESS-CRITICAL -- a suppressed first stop/escalate or first operator_required is a MISSED ESCALATION) live in _never_suppress_reason() short-circuiting decide() BEFORE any cooldown logic AND tolerant of a store read failure (fail-open per doc-10 Edge Cases): a direct operator answer -> operator_requested; a first stop/escalate for a NEW failure signature OR a first operator_required for a NEW route -> material_change; all always send regardless of any prior state row. A non-exception background digest still surfaces a store failure as a typed DigestDedupeStoreError for the doc-10 fail-quiet split. The store is supervisor-OWNED (exposes ZERO CONTROL_PLANE_WRITER_METHODS; assert_no_control_plane_writer passes; the supervisor-digest-state:/supervisor-digest-audit: prefixes were already pre-registered in read_only.py SUPERVISOR_OWNED_AUDIT_KEY_PREFIXES) -- on the allowed side of the Slice-10c-1 read-only contract. The legacy SupervisorSlackDigestDecisionStore stays byte-for-byte unchanged.

decision: SPLIT Slice 10d into 10d-1 (the store + the two tables + the decision logic -- THIS iteration) and 10d-2 (the SupervisorSlackService send/suppress routing -- next iteration). STATUS.md flagged the split possibility; the store/schema is a self-contained persistence + pure-decision-logic unit fully verifiable in isolation with the real-Postgres fixture, while the routing is a genuine cross-component behavior change reopening the watch_and_digest loop that must get its own independent review. Per the IMPLEMENTATION_PROMPT loop discipline (one verified sub-deliverable per iteration; prefer a smaller fully-verified chunk over a large shaky one) 10d-1 is the first coherent verifiable chunk -- the entire doc-10 send/suppress decision procedure (the 30-min cooldown, the coalescing, the never-suppress exceptions) lives in decide() and is exhaustively tested here without the Slack service.
alternatives_rejected: Deliver all of 10d (store + tables + the SupervisorSlackService routing) in one iteration -- rejected: the routing reopens the watch_and_digest loop (a genuine behavior change that must NOT defer its review to the slice-end loop) and bundling it with the new persistence risks a large shaky chunk; the store + decision logic is independently fully verifiable.

Reuse / extend the existing legacy SupervisorSlackDigestDecisionStore + its supervisor_slack_digest_* tables instead of adding new ones -- rejected: doc 10 Slack Dedupe And Suppression explicitly names the new tables supervisor_digest_state / supervisor_digest_audit with a DIFFERENT column contract (last_snapshot_version/suppressed_count/last_digest_payload vs. the legacy status/signature_hash/semantic_signature_hash) and a different dedupe key (the stable JSON digest over SupervisorDigestKey); extending the legacy store would mean rewriting working code (feedback_no_refactor) and would not match the doc-10 contract.

Put the dedupe state in an artifacts row -- rejected: doc 10 is explicit -- They are audit state, not execution authority. Do not use artifacts for dedupe state; artifacts may be projected for operator review only after the table write succeeds.

Re-export SupervisorDigestDedupeStore from supervisor/__init__.py -- rejected: the Slice-10c precedent (classifier_mapping / read_only) keeps new additive leaf modules reachable via their module path and NOT re-exported from __init__.py; only the Pydantic models are re-exported (the models.py precedent).
next_action: Slice 10d-1 gets its own independent review (the KEY vector: supervisor/dashboard safety -- the dedupe store never drops an operator answer or a first safety stop; bounded reads). Then Slice 10d-2: route ALL background Slack digests in SupervisorSlackService (supervisor/slack.py watch_and_digest) through decide()/record_sent/record_suppressed before calling the Slack client -- that seam derives is_operator_answer / new_failure_signature / new_operator_route from the typed snapshot and gets its own independent review. Then the doc-10 step-4 evidence_mode wiring, the public-dashboard outbox projection + startup assertions, the atomic landing + full test matrix + the slice-end six-vector review + formal Slice 10 acceptance.

summary: Slice 10d-1 REVIEW (the supervisor Slack dedupe store + the two supervisor-owned tables) -- VERDICT CLEAN: 0 P1, 0 P2, 0 new P3 across all seven vectors. Vector 1 the never-suppress exceptions (CORRECTNESS-CRITICAL, the KEY vector) PASS -- decide() (digest_dedupe.py:254-290) calls _never_suppress_reason() at :259 FIRST; that method (:362-412) is a pure synchronous function taking only key + the three flags with ZERO table reads; when it returns non-None the never-suppress branch (:266-290) returns should_send=True UNCONDITIONALLY and its only table touch (_load_state at :275, purely informational for prior_digest_id/suppressed_count) is wrapped in try/except DigestDedupeStoreError (:274-283) degrading to prior=None; the non-exception background _load_state at :296 runs ONLY after the never-suppress branch is bypassed. All three arms verified to fire under a TOTAL Postgres outage: Arm A operator answer->operator_requested/send (:391); Arm B first stop/escalate (recommended_action in {stop/escalate,stop_escalate}, case+whitespace-tolerant via .strip().lower(), AND new_failure_signature=True)->material_change/send (:401-403); Arm C first operator_required (classification==operator_required AND new_operator_route=True)->material_change/send (:409-410). _fetchrow/_fetch/_execute (:677-718) re-wrap EVERY non-DigestDedupeStoreError exception as DigestDedupeStoreError so the only exception that can escape _load_state is the one the never-suppress try/except catches -- a bare RuntimeError cannot leak past the fail-open send; there is NO path where one of the three is suppressed. The short-circuit fires before background-idempotency (rule 3) and the cooldown (rule 4) -- correct, doc-10s never-suppress rules are unconditional. A non-exception background digest correctly RE-RAISES the typed DigestDedupeStoreError for the doc-10 fail-quiet split. Vector 2 suppression+coalescing PASS -- SUPPRESSION_COOLDOWN_SECONDS=30*60 is a module constant; the strict moment-last_sent_at<SUPPRESSION_COOLDOWN (:334) makes the boundary inclusive of a send (29m59s suppressed, exactly 30m sends, 30m01s sends); suppressed_within_cooldown carries suppressed_count=prior+1; record_suppressed COALESCEs last_sent_at (:603-605) so a suppression never looks like a send; post-cooldown a coalesced send carries the count (coalesced when prior>0 else material_change); record_sent resets to 0; verified on real TIMESTAMPTZ; a naive last_sent_at is coerced by _as_utc and still suppresses. Vector 3 the two tables PASS -- supervisor_digest_state (13 cols) + supervisor_digest_audit (14 cols) match the doc-10 column contracts field-by-field incl. types/nullability/defaults, the (feature_id,dedupe_key) unique constraint, the 2+3 doc-10 indexes, both FKs; DISTINCT from the legacy supervisor_slack_digest_* tables (additive); DB-valid against real Postgres; dedupe state lives ONLY in these tables, no artifacts row. Vector 4 the read-only contract PASS -- assert_no_control_plane_writer passes; the store public surface AND its full name set incl. private/dunder have ZERO overlap with CONTROL_PLANE_WRITER_METHODS (28) and FEATURE_TIMELINE_WRITER_METHODS (7); the prefixes supervisor-digest-state:/supervisor-digest-audit: are registered in read_only.py SUPERVISOR_OWNED_AUDIT_KEY_PREFIXES (:228-229); digest_dedupe.py imports only supervisor.models + stdlib (stable_json re-implemented locally) -- a clean supervisor leaf, a supervisor-OWNED audit/dedupe writer on the allowed side of the Slice-10c-1 enforcement. Vector 5 model conformance PASS -- SupervisorDigestKey + SupervisorDigestDecision transcribed verbatim from the doc-10 code block, the 6-value reason Literal matches, both re-exported from __init__.py. Vector 6 bounded reads PASS -- every read is feature-scoped + keyed; audit_history clamps limit to _AUDIT_HISTORY_CAP=50; payloads size-capped, no artifact bodies. Vector 7 test honesty PASS -- 31 genuine tests; the _FakePool faithfully models the upsert + COALESCE(last_sent_at) + the append-only audit; the never-suppress arms tested positively AND negatively (a repeat without the new-signature/route flag IS suppressible); 11 real-Postgres tests genuinely RAN (localhost:5431 up, 0 skips via -rs); tests/supervisor/ GREEN at 334 (303 baseline + 31, no test weakened).

decision: Slice 10d-1 REVIEW returns CLEAN -- 0 P1, 0 P2, 0 new P3 across all seven vectors. The CORRECTNESS-CRITICAL never-suppress vector (1) PASSES: the _never_suppress_reason short-circuit is fail-open, runs before any state read, re-wraps every store exception as the typed DigestDedupeStoreError the never-suppress try/except catches, and genuinely fires for all three exception cases even under a total store outage -- there is no path where an operator answer / a first stop/escalate / a first operator_required is suppressed. The read-only-contract vector (4) PASSES: the store is genuinely supervisor-owned (zero CONTROL_PLANE_WRITER_METHODS, assert_no_control_plane_writer passes, the audit-key prefixes are on the allowed side of the Slice-10c-1 enforcement). Slice 10d-1 is finalized and STATUS.md is advanced to Slice 10d-2 (wire SupervisorDigestDedupeStore into SupervisorSlackService).
alternatives_rejected: Flag the never-suppress short-circuit firing before the background-idempotency rule (rule 3) as a P3 -- rejected: doc-10s never-suppress rules are unconditional (Never suppress direct operator answers, first stop/escalate for a new failure signature, or first operator_required for a new typed route); a never-suppress digest is by definition NOT a background digest, so the (feature_id,group_idx,dedupe_key,snapshot_version) background-idempotency rule does not apply to it; sending a first escalation even when the same snapshot_version was sent is correct and conservative.

Flag the absence of a SupervisorSlackService caller as a P2/P3 -- rejected: Slice 10d was deliberately SPLIT (journaled in both 10d-1 IMPLEMENTATION and STATUS.md); 10d-1 ships the store + decision logic, 10d-2 ships the watch_and_digest routing as a separately-reviewed behavior change; the dormant store is intentional, not a defect.

Flag SupervisorDigestKey list fields defaulting to Field(default_factory=list) as a model-conformance P3 -- rejected: doc-10s shape shows no default but a defaulted empty list is a strict superset of the doc shape and harmless; no behavior depends on the field being required.

summary: Slice 10d-2 IMPLEMENTATION -- wired SupervisorDigestDedupeStore into SupervisorSlackService.watch_and_digest. The loop now polls -> the unchanged legacy _digest_packet_to_send_durable pre-filter -> the new _route_background_digest, which ALWAYS calls SupervisorDigestDedupeStore.decide() before the Slack client. The two adapter.post_blocks calls (the stale-Codex card + the regular status digest) now live ONLY inside _post_background_digest, unreachable unless _decide_background_digest returned should_send=True -- a direct background send is structurally impossible. On should_send=False -> record_suppressed + no send; on should_send=True -> post FIRST, then record_sent (a failed send re-raises before record_sent and re-decides as first_seen next poll). The SupervisorDigestKey is built from the typed packet (_build_digest_key); the three never-suppress flags are derived EXACTLY -- new_failure_signature / new_operator_route = store.get_state(key) is None (the key digest folds the failure signature hashes AND the route, so a genuinely new signature/route yields a brand-new key with no prior state row), is_operator_answer is always False (direct answers flow through answer_question and never reach this routing). decide() store-read failure applies the doc-10 fail-open (escalation) / fail-quiet (background duplicate) split via _is_never_suppress_candidate; _derive_never_suppress_flags degrades conservatively to first-seen on a store outage. The legacy SupervisorSlackDigestDecisionStore + supervisor_slack_digest_* tables are byte-for-byte unchanged (additive); the new dedupe store resolves off the same pool. tests/supervisor/ GREEN at 351 passed (334 baseline + 17 new Slice-10d-2 tests; 1 test updated for the genuine behavior change with every original assertion kept + 1 added, no weakening). Hygiene clean.

summary: Slice 10d-2 REVIEW (the Slack dedupe routing wired into SupervisorSlackService.watch_and_digest) -- VERDICT CLEAN: 0 P1, 0 P2, 0 new P3 across all seven vectors; one non-blocking carried P3-10d-2-1. Vector 1 -- ALL background digests route through decide() (THE KEY VECTOR) PASS, verified STRUCTURALLY: grep of every post_blocks call site in slack.py yields exactly four -- :1782 and :1821 both inside _post_background_digest, :2881 and :2907 both inside SupervisorSlackRouter.handle_action (a separate operator-action path, not watch_and_digest); the post_message/update_message calls are all in SupervisorSlackProgress (operator Q&A) or handle_action. watch_and_digest (:1619) calls ONLY _route_background_digest; _route_background_digest (:1654) reaches a Slack send ONLY via _post_background_digest (:1725), called ONLY after the if not send: ... return guard (:1706-1723) -- i.e. only on should_send=True from _decide_background_digest, which ALWAYS calls SupervisorDigestDedupeStore.decide() (:1973) or applies the doc-10 fail-open/fail-quiet split on a typed DigestDedupeStoreError. There is NO branch where a background digest reaches post_blocks without decide(); a direct background send is structurally impossible. Vector 2 send/record ordering PASS: should_send=False -> _record_dedupe_suppressed (record_suppressed) + _record_digest_suppressed + return with NO send; should_send=True -> _post_background_digest FIRST then _record_dedupe_sent (record_sent); a failed Slack send -- _post_background_digest records the failed legacy claim and RE-RAISES (:1787-1793, :1826-1832), the exception propagates through _route_background_digest (no try/except) and is caught by watch_and_digest except Exception (:1650), so record_sent is NEVER reached on a failed send and the next poll re-decide()s the key as first_seen (a new, not a lost, digest); the _DIGEST_NOT_POSTED sentinel (:1217, a unique object()) is returned only on an intentional non-send and the identity check message_ts is _DIGEST_NOT_POSTED (:1730) cannot collide with a real string Slack ts. Vector 3 never-suppress flag derivation (KEY VECTOR) PASS: compute_dedupe_key (digest_dedupe.py:112) folds failure_signature_hashes + recommended_route into the digest, so a genuinely new signature/route yields a brand-new dedupe key with NO prior supervisor_digest_state row; _derive_never_suppress_flags (:1868) derives new_failure_signature AND new_operator_route as exactly get_state(key) is None -- the exact minimal derivation: a first digest of a new signature/route is a first-seen key (never under-fires -> always sends), a repeat of the SAME signature/route reuses the key, finds the prior row, derives False, is suppressible (never over-fires -> no operator flood); is_operator_answer is hardcoded False because direct operator answers flow through answer_question/evaluate_action_request and never reach _route_background_digest; on a DigestDedupeStoreError the prior-state read degrades CONSERVATIVELY to first_seen=True (:1915-1922). Vector 4 the legacy in-process throttle (the P2-watch) JUDGED -- NOT a P2, at most a P3: _digest_packet_to_send (:2213) is the unchanged legacy pre-filter; a non-OBSERVE digest (incl. a first stop/escalate) arriving within min_digest_interval_seconds (default 120s) of the prior digest is parked into _pending_digest_packet and returns None (:2235-2247), but pending_due (:2215-2218) re-emits it on a later poll and decide()'s never-suppress arm then fires -- it is a DELAY, never a drop; doc 10 Slack Dedupe And Suppression says Never SUPPRESS a first stop/escalate -- the operative verb is suppress (drop/coalesce-away) not delay, and a one-poll-interval delay still SENDS the digest; doc 10 Refactoring Steps step 7 only requires routing produced digests through send/suppress decisions before the Slack client, which the new gate does. decision: Slice 10d-2 REVIEW returns CLEAN -- 0 P1, 0 P2, 0 new P3 across all seven vectors. The three KEY vectors PASS with explicit verdicts: vector 1 (no-bypass routing) -- watch_and_digest can NO LONGER send a background digest without decide(), verified structurally via every post_blocks call site; vector 3 (never-suppress flag derivation) -- a new signature/route always sends and a repeat is suppressible because the dedupe key folds the failure signature hashes and the typed route so new_failure_signature/new_operator_route reduce to get_state(key) is None, and direct operator answers do NOT route through background suppression; vector 4 (the legacy-throttle delay) -- a one-poll-interval DELAY of a first escalation is acceptable for 10d-2 scope because the digest still SENDS (doc 10 forbids suppression, not delay).
